- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The claim says the 4.06 mean score *provides* good quality of test cases in ASD.

Modality: Span says experts agreed that a management quality factor *could* provide good quality of test cases. Claim asserts that the mean score *provides* good quality of test cases. This changes modality and meaning slightly. Also, the span says the *factor* could provide good quality, whereas the claim says the *mean score* provides good quality. The mean score is just a measure of agreement, not the thing that provides the quality.

Let's look at the span: "The respondents (experts) agreed that a management quality factor could provide good quality of test cases in ASD with a mean score of 4.06"
This means the respondents' agreement (that the factor could provide good quality) had a mean score of 4.06.
The claim: "4.06 mean score on rating scale in management quality factor provides good quality of test cases in ASD (expert respondents)"
The claim seems to assert the mean score itself provides good quality of test cases, whereas the span says the factor could provide it. Also "could provide" vs "provides" (modality change).

Therefore, the claim changes modality ("could provide" -> "provides") and subject (the factor -> mean score). So it is NOT_ENTAILED.

247	def reverify(b: CardBundle) -> list[str]:
248	    """BEFORE ANY LLM CALL. Every PRIMARY and every CORROBORATING binding, re-resolved from the bytes.
249	
250	    Not "on the way out". Not "in a preflight somebody may run". HERE, before a single token is spent,
251	    because a card that cannot prove its bytes must never reach a writer's context window — the writer
252	    will faithfully report it, and everything downstream will then be checking a fabrication against
253	    itself.
254	    """
255	    ok, bad = [], []
256	    # ITERATE A SNAPSHOT. The loop body ADDS corroborating cards to `b.cards`, and iterating a dict
257	    # while inserting into it is a RuntimeError. It does not fire today only because no card in this
258	    # bundle carries a corroborating source — i.e. the crash is waiting for the first one that does,
259	    # which is precisely the code path we least want to discover in production.
260	    for cid in list(b.cards):
261	        r = b.resolve(cid)
262	        (ok if r.ok else bad).append(cid if r.ok else f'{cid}: {r.refusal}')
263	        # A CORROBORATING SOURCE IS A CITATION. It gets the same chain, independently — it is exactly as
264	        # capable of naming a document its span never came from as the primary is.
265	        for j, cs in enumerate(b.cards[cid].get('corroborating_sources') or []):
266	            sub = dict(cs)
267	            sub.setdefault('id', f'{cid}#corr{j}')
268	            if sub['id'] not in b.cards:
269	                b.cards[sub['id']] = sub
270	            rc = b.resolve(sub['id'])
271	            if not rc.ok:
272	                bad.append(f'{cid} corroborating[{j}]: {rc.refusal}')
273	    return bad

338	class CardBundle:
339	    """ONE card lane. ONE graph. ONE policy. THE SEAM IS GONE.
340	
341	    There were TWO DISCONNECTED CARD LANES: the miner wrote `evidence_cards_v2.json` and the composer
342	    read `evidence_cards.json` — a file whose cards carry no manifestation, no hash and no expression,
343	    and whose four Frey & Osborne cards were mined out of an ORA LANDING PAGE. The composer could not
344	    have detected that, because the lane it read had nothing in it to detect with.
345	
346	    A bundle is constructed from an EXPLICIT path plus the graph, and it REFUSES to exist if the bytes
347	    on disk are not the bytes it was promised.
348	    """
349	
350	    def __init__(self, cards: list[dict], graph: P.Graph,
351	                 policy: P.SourcePolicy = P.JOURNAL_ONLY,
352	                 cards_sha: str = '', graph_sha: str = '', ledger_sha: str = ''):
353	        self.graph = graph
354	        self.policy = policy
355	        self.cards_sha = cards_sha
356	        self.graph_sha = graph_sha
357	        self.ledger_sha = ledger_sha
358	        # A DUPLICATE CARD ID IS A REFUSAL, NOT AN OVERWRITE.
359	        #
360	        # This dict used to be filled with `self.cards[cid] = c`, and the miner's id is
361	        # `{doi}:{span_start}-{span_end}` — so TWO FINDINGS MINED FROM ONE SPAN COLLIDE. On the first
362	        # real bundle, 13 of 52 cards were silently overwritten: the composer printed "52 cards, 0
363	        # refused" and had 39. The evidence did not fail a check. IT LEFT WITHOUT ONE, which is the
364	        # quieter half of every defect in this file's header. And a sentence citing a colliding id
365	        # names an AMBIGUOUS card: same span, different claim, different act, different table row.
366	        self.cards: dict[str, dict] = {}
367	        dupes: dict[str, int] = {}
368	        for c in cards:
369	            cid = c.get('id')
370	            if not cid:
371	                raise ValueError('a card with no `id` cannot be cited by a sentence, and is REFUSED')
372	            if cid in self.cards:
373	                dupes[cid] = dupes.get(cid, 1) + 1
374	                continue
375	            self.cards[cid] = c
376	        if dupes:
377	            n = sum(dupes.values()) - len(dupes)
378	            raise ValueError(
379	                f'{len(dupes)} DUPLICATE CARD ID(S) ({n} cards would be silently dropped) — a bundle '
380	                f'whose ids are ambiguous is not a bundle, and a sentence citing one of them names no '
381	                f'determinate evidence. Offenders: {sorted(dupes)[:3]}')
382	        self._cache: dict[str, Resolution] = {}
383	        # Facet derivation is deterministic but regex-heavy. Verdict enumeration reuses the same cards
384	        # across hundreds of pairs, so cache by the resolved receipt object AND contract identity. If a
385	        # caller clears `_cache` after changing graph state, the new Resolution identity cannot hit this.
386	        self._premise_cache: dict[tuple[str, int, int], Premise] = {}
387	        # Every surname AND venue the bundle knows. Used ONLY NEGATIVELY — to refuse a source name in a
388	        # lane that is not allowed to have one. We never infer WHICH source from a surname again.
389	        #

392	        # sub-4-char surname ("Wu", "Ng") was invisible, as was a bare surname when the author was stored

395	        self._source_words: set[str] = set()      # single tokens, matched at word boundaries

397	        for c in self.cards.values():

399	                self._index_person(a)

406	    def _index_person(self, name: str) -> None:
407	        """Index the ways a byline can NAME a source, and ONLY those. NEVER a function word.
408	
409	        A PERSON is named by their whole name or by their SURNAME alone ("Wu", "Ng", "Acemoglu"). A
410	        CORPORATE byline ("on behalf of the INSPIRING Project Consortium") is named by its WHOLE phrase
411	        and by an explicit acronym — but never by "the"/"of"/"on"/"behalf" or every component token.
412	        The old code added EVERY token of every byline, so `the` and `of` entered `_source_words` and a
413	        valid planner verdict was refused `OWNED_NAMES_A_SOURCE: 'the'` before its proof could matter.
414	        """
415	        raw = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', (name or '').strip())
416	        n = raw.lower()
417	        if not n:
418	            return
419	
420	        # (0) THE WHOLE BYLINE is always a phrase the reviewer's own prose may not reproduce.
421	        if ' ' in n:
422	            self._source_phrases.add(n)
423	        elif len(n) >= 2:
424	            self._source_words.add(n)                     # a one-token name: "Wu", "Ng", "Acemoglu"
425	
426	        # (1) A CORPORATE/GROUP byline. Whole phrase (above) + explicit ACRONYMS only. Never a token.
427	        toks_l = re.findall(r"[a-z][a-z.'’\-]*[a-z]", n)   # drop bare initials ("j."), keep "wu"
428	        if any(t in _CORPORATE_MARKERS for t in toks_l) or 'on behalf of' in n:
429	            for tok in re.findall(r'\b[A-Z]{2,}\b', raw):  # ALL-CAPS acronym in the ORIGINAL bytes
430	                if tok.lower() not in _BYLINE_FUNCTION_WORDS:
431	                    self._source_words.add(tok.lower())
432	            return
433	
434	        # (2) AN INDIVIDUAL PERSON. Index the SURNAME, and only the surname.
435	        if ',' in n:                                       # "Surname, Given [Middle]"
436	            surname_toks = [t for t in re.findall(r"[a-z][a-z.'’\-]*[a-z]", n.split(',', 1)[0])
437	                            if t not in _NAME_PARTICLES and t not in _BYLINE_FUNCTION_WORDS]
438	            for t in surname_toks:
439	                if len(t) >= 2:
440	                    self._source_words.add(t)
441	            return
442	        # "Given … Surname": the TERMINAL token is the surname; a leading particle ("van") is not.
443	        surname_toks = [t for t in toks_l
444	                        if t not in _NAME_PARTICLES and t not in _BYLINE_FUNCTION_WORDS]
445	        if surname_toks and len(surname_toks[-1]) >= 2:
446	            self._source_words.add(surname_toks[-1])

448	    def _index_venue(self, venue: str) -> None:
449	        """A venue can be named whole ("the American Economic Review") or, when it is one distinctive
450	        word ("Science", "Nature", "Technovation"), by that word. We do NOT add single tokens of a
451	        multi-word venue — "economic" or "review" are common words and would refuse honest prose."""
452	        v = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', (venue or '').strip().lower())
453	        if not v:
454	            return
455	        core = re.sub(r'^the\s+', '', v)
456	        toks = core.split(' ')
457	        if len(toks) == 1 and len(toks[0]) >= 4:
458	            self._source_words.add(toks[0])
459	        else:
460	            self._source_phrases.add(v)
461	            if core != v:
462	                self._source_phrases.add(core)

464	    # -- THE ONLY DOOR ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
465	    def resolve(self, card_id: str) -> Resolution:
466	        """sentence -> card -> bound span -> manifestation_id + content_hash -> permitted expression
467	        -> attribution.
468	
469	        EVERY step is re-derived from the graph HERE, at use time. Nothing stored on the card is
470	        trusted: not the attribution, not the permitted set, not the expression, not the hash.
471	
472	        `verify_span()` is called FIRST — before any field of the card is read for any purpose — because
473	        a card whose bytes do not verify is not a weak card, it is NOT A CARD, and reading its `venue`
474	        to make a decision is how a landing page became a peer-reviewed article.
475	        """
476	        if card_id in self._cache:
477	            return self._cache[card_id]
478	        r = self._resolve(card_id)
479	        self._cache[card_id] = r
480	        return r
481	
482	    def _resolve(self, card_id: str) -> Resolution:
483	        c = self.cards.get(card_id)
484	        if c is None:
485	            return Resolution(False, card_id, refusal='NO_SUCH_CARD_IN_BUNDLE')
486	
487	        binding = _binding_from_card(c)
488	        if not binding:
489	            return Resolution(False, card_id, refusal='CARD_IS_UNBOUND (no manifestation_id/content_hash '
490	                                                      '/span offsets — a DOI names a work, and a work has '
491	                                                      'no bytes)')
492	        # ---- 1. THE BYTES. Untrusted input; re-check offsets, hash, and the text itself.
493	        if not self.graph.verify_span(binding):
494	            return Resolution(False, card_id, refusal=f'SPAN_DOES_NOT_VERIFY against '
495	                                                      f'{binding["manifestation_id"]}')
496	        mid = binding['manifestation_id']
497	        m = self.graph.manifestations[mid]
498	
499	        # ---- 2. THE POLICY. What, if anything, may THIS SPAN name?
500	        #         Sol V9 §4: the question is asked about the BINDING, not about the manifestation. A
501	        #         manifestation-wide answer would hand THIS span whatever permission any OTHER span in
502	        #         the same document had earned — and the spans peer review rewrote sit in the very same
503	        #         document as the spans it left alone. Permission is per span or it is fiction.
504	        att = self.graph.resolve_attribution(binding, self.policy)
505	        if not att.admitted:
506	            return Resolution(False, card_id, refusal=f'SOURCE_POLICY_REFUSES: {att.refusal}')
507	        # THE IDENTITY GATE IS THE AUTHORITY — not the card's venue/doi/authors/attribution. The
508	        # resolver's structured verdict must be INTERNALLY CONSISTENT and name a POSITIVELY PROVEN
509	        # identity. `att.admitted` alone is not enough: a future edit or an in-memory tamper could
510	        # leave `admitted=True` beside a stale disposition or an identity outside the allowlist, and
511	        # that inconsistent tuple must fail closed rather than launder a source. This IMPORTS the
512	        # canonical allowlist (`event_ledger.IDENTITY_PROVEN`); it does not copy it, and it adds NO
513	        # venue allowlist — a venue string is metadata, never identity proof.
514	        from event_ledger import IDENTITY_PROVEN as _IDOK  # noqa: PLC0415
515	        if not (att.disposition == P.DISPOSITION_ADMIT
516	                and att.reason_code == P.RC_ADMITTED
517	                and att.identity_verdict in _IDOK
518	                and att.names_expression_id is not None):
519	            return Resolution(False, card_id,
520	                              refusal=('IDENTITY_HANDOFF_INCONSISTENT (admitted but the structured '
521	                                       f'verdict is not a proven ADMIT): disposition={att.disposition!r} '
522	                                       f'reason_code={att.reason_code!r} '
523	                                       f'identity_verdict={att.identity_verdict!r} '
524	                                       f'names_expression_id={att.names_expression_id!r}'))
525	
526	        # ---- 3. THE CARD'S OWN STORED TARGET MUST BE THE ONE THE GRAPH RESOLVES *NOW*.
527	        #         A stored target is a cache, and a cache that is not checked is how a stale permission
528	        #         keeps naming a journal it was never allowed to name.
529	        stored = c.get('attribution_target_expression_id')
530	        if stored and stored != att.names_expression_id:
531	            return Resolution(False, card_id,
532	                              refusal=f'STALE_ATTRIBUTION_TARGET: card says {stored!r}, the graph '
533	                                      f'resolves {att.names_expression_id!r} under `{self.policy.name}`')
534	
535	        # ---- 4. THE ATTRIBUTION IS RENDERED FROM THE GRAPH. The card's own string is never read.
536	        expr = self.graph.expressions[att.names_expression_id]
537	        work = self.graph.works[expr.work_id]
538	        prose = render_attribution(work, expr)
539	        if not prose:
540	            return Resolution(False, card_id,
541	                              refusal=f'EXPRESSION_KIND_IS_NOT_RENDERABLE_AS_A_CITATION: {expr.kind!r}')
542	
543	        return Resolution(
544	            True, card_id, manifestation_id=mid, content_hash=binding['content_hash'],
545	            expression_id=binding.get('expression_id') or m.expression_id,
546	            names_expression_id=att.names_expression_id,
547	            span=binding['text'], span_start=binding['span_start'], span_end=binding['span_end'],
548	            work_id=m.work_id, attribution=prose, card=c)

550	    def admitted_ids(self) -> list[str]:
551	        return [cid for cid in self.cards if self.resolve(cid).ok]

556	        This is not `_cited_cards()` coming back in through a window. That function asked "WHICH source
557	        is this sentence about?" and answered with a guess, then gated the sentence against the guess.
558	        This one asks "does this text name ANY source at all?" and uses the answer only to say NO. There

562	        corpus multi-word venue, or a well-known journal/reporter the model typed from memory though the
563	        corpus never held it (Sol's "Science"); (3) a reporting-attribution construction ("<X> reports
564	        that ..."), which names a source for ANY invented venue, not only the ones we can enumerate.
565	        """
566	        raw = text or ''
567	        low = ' ' + re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', raw.lower()) + ' '

855	# MEASURED ON THE SHIPPED REPORT: 222 card slots drawn from 82 cards. ONE finding narrated EIGHT TIMES.
856	# 41 exact repetitions. ~1,500-2,000 words of pure restatement — in a document whose lowest criterion is
857	# paragraph cohesion and whose judge wrote "fragmented narrative".
858	#
859	# THE CAUSE IS IN THIS FILE, AND IT IS `_select()`. Every subsection independently scores every admitted
860	# card by lexical fit and takes the top 12. A canonical finding — the one with the big number and the
861	# familiar vocabulary — scores in the top 12 of EIGHT subsections, so it is DEALT to eight writers, and
862	# eight writers each faithfully narrate it. No writer misbehaved. Nobody ever told them it was spent.
863	#
864	# `fact_use_ledger.plan_bundles()` deals every subsection a DELIBERATELY DIFFERENT bundle: narration is
865	# a PARTITION over findings (R1 — narrated in full exactly once), reuse is granted only in a NEW
866	# ANALYTICAL ROLE that must ADD something (R2/R3 — mechanism / contrast / boundary / method / implication),
867	# and everything else degrades to an OWNED BACKWARD REFERENCE that POINTS at the fact without SAYING it
868	# again (R4).
869	#
870	# IDENTITY IS KEYED ON sha1(THE VERBATIM SPAN), NEVER ON THE MODEL'S `claim`: "identity keyed on model
871	# prose is identity the model can forge by rewording" — a writer could otherwise launder a spent fact
872	# into a fresh one just by paraphrasing it.
873	#
874	# WHAT THE LEDGER DOES NOT DO: it does not touch the BASKET. Corroborating sources stay retained, cited
875	# and counted (`Cluster.corroborators`); the ledger governs RHETORICAL REUSE ONLY. And it is NOT a
876	# "one card, one section" partition of the EVIDENCE — Sol rejected that explicitly, because it starves
877	# the theory and synthesis sections of the canonical findings they exist to reason over. Those sections
878	# still get the big findings; they get them in a role that does a NEW JOB.

881	class Licence:
882	    """What THIS subsection may do with each card. The unit of authority is (card, subsection)."""

884	    __slots__ = ('narrate', 'new_role', 'backref')

886	    def __init__(self, narrate=(), new_role=(), backref=()):
887	        self.narrate = list(narrate)                 # card_ids: state in full, WITH the figure

893	        """The ONLY cards this subsection may put in an ATTRIBUTED clause."""

901	    only place the two vocabularies meet, and the mapping is deterministic: `admitted` is ordered, so
902	    where two cards carry the same finding (same work, same bytes — 13 of them do in this bundle) the
903	    first one always wins and the duplicate is not separately narratable. That is not a loss: a second

**The flywheel turned, and the answer was not where anyone was looking.**

Fable's gate **refuted my thesis**, and I verified the refutation against source myself. I had argued the ~51%-alien corpus was what `strict_verify` was killing. It isn't: `strict_verify(draft_text, evidence_pool)` never receives the research question, and its judge is `judge(sentence, span)` — *"does the SPAN entail the SENTENCE."* A sentence faithfully written from a **real but alien** source is entailed by its span and is **kept**. Off-topic evidence produces irrelevant prose, never *dropped* prose. It cannot be what `kept_fraction` measures.

What the gate found underneath is bigger, and I confirmed all three claims with my own greps:

**There is no length governor on this code path at all.** `PG_TARGET_TOTAL_WORDS=8000`, the expansion passes, the per-section word cap — every one is consumed only by `agents/synthesizer.py` and `synthesis/section_writer.py`, and `multi_section_generator.py` **imports neither** (grep: 0 hits). Compose calls `generate_multi_section_report` directly. All that word-budget config in `.env` is **dead code**. Report length has been purely emergent this whole time: `sections × rule-#8's sentence target × ~30 words`. The project has been tuning retrieval while its report length was an accident of one prompt line — and rule #8's *"Target 10-18 sentences"*, written as a floor-raiser against the model's natural 6–8, is binding as a **ceiling** exactly where evidence is richest (rich sections already render 16–18, the top of the band).

**Rank7 (`33108d8`)** makes that band a lever: `PG_SECTION_SENTENCE_TARGET` and `PG_SECTION_DISTINCT_SOURCES`, applied at the single selector seam so it can't half-fire across the two templates. Default-unset returns the *same object* — byte-identical, so nothing moves until an arm opts in. My own test caught a defect mid-build: rewriting rule #8 alone left the Mechanism rule still back-referencing *"rule #8 target of 10-18 sentences"*, which would have handed the model **28-36 and 10-18 in the same prompt**. Now rewritten, with a hard assert that no stale band survives.

Rule #9 moves *with* rule #8 deliberately — B11 same-span dedup keeps only one sentence per distinct `(ev_id, start, end)` footprint, so extra sentences that re-word an already-cited span get silently deleted. **The lever is "cite more distinct sources," not "write more sentences."** That distinction is the difference between this working and it being another no-op.

Two things worth flagging to you directly:

- **The experiment you specified (source-eligibility firewall on tier/peer-reviewed/English) would not have worked.** 56 of the junk rows are **T1/T2 — the top credibility tiers**: a gradeless-learning RCT, a mass-deworming study, a Wiley governance journal. A tier-based firewall keeps every one. Relevance, not credibility, is the broken axis. I did not build it.
- **GitHub push is still blocked** — no PAT, no SSH key, no `gh` on the box. Backups are local bundles (`flywheel-v1-33108d8.bundle`). Paste a token and I'll push immediately.

Next, once the A/B lands: the 3-arm compose test (raised target / raised target + `PG_WRITER_TOPN_EV_PER_SECTION=20` / control). The non-fakeable signal is the `B11 collapsed` counter — if words stay flat while B11 explodes, the ceiling is span diversity, not the prompt, and I'll say so rather than claim a win.

def _log_reasoning(prompt: str, r) -> None:
    """Persist + surface the glm reasoning trace for one call. Best-effort; swallows every error."""
    try:
        reasoning = getattr(r, 'reasoning', None)
        if not reasoning:
            return
        rtoks = getattr(r, 'reasoning_tokens', 0) or 0
        tag = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', (prompt or '')[:60]).strip()
        head = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', reasoning).strip()
        # LIVE marker on the compose log — one line per call, so a live tail shows the model thinking
        # and shows the LAST reasoning line before any freeze.
        print(f'  [glm reasoning: {rtoks} tokens] {head[:80]}...', flush=True)
        try:
            _REASONING_LOG.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
            with open(_REASONING_LOG, 'a', encoding='utf-8') as fh:
                fh.write(f'\n===== {time.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")} | reasoning_tokens={rtoks} | '
                         f'prompt[:60]={tag!r} =====\n{reasoning}\n')
        except Exception:
            pass
    except Exception:
        pass

        print(f'  {k:24}: {meta[k]}')
    return 0


if __name__ == '__main__':
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument('--cards', type=Path, default=ROOT / 'outputs' / 'evidence_cards_bound.json')
    ap.add_argument('--graph', type=Path, default=ROOT / 'outputs' / 'provenance_graph.json')
    ap.add_argument('--ledger', type=Path, default=ROOT / 'outputs' / 'event_ledger.jsonl')
    ap.add_argument('--policy', default='journal_articles_only', choices=sorted(POLICIES))
    ap.add_argument('--expect-cards-sha', default='')
    ap.add_argument('--expect-graph-sha', default='')
    ap.add_argument('--write', action='store_true')
    ap.add_argument('--dry', action='store_true', help='no LLM: prove the bindings and the AST only')
    a = ap.parse_args()
    # DIAGNOSTIC WATCHDOG (env-gated, off by default): dump ALL thread stacks every N seconds so a
    # post-render freeze names its exact line in the log. Harmless when COMPOSE_FAULTDUMP is unset.
    if os.environ.get('COMPOSE_FAULTDUMP'):
        import faulthandler
        import signal as _sig
        # On-demand: `kill -USR1 <pid>` dumps ALL thread stacks to stderr (the log). Safer than the
        # repeating timer (which can race C-level teardown into a segfault).
        faulthandler.register(_sig.SIGUSR1, all_threads=True)
    # THE COMPILED CONTRACT — the SAME `research_contract.compile_contract` the miner ran on this query.
    # It drives the query-derived title/abstract/methods prose AND (via `_planner_contract`) the planner's
    # span-facet vocabulary. Taken from PG_RESEARCH_QUESTION; absent/failed compile -> None (subject-free
    # generic frame, empty-facet planner) — never a hardcoded task subject.
    compiled = None
    _q = (os.environ.get('PG_RESEARCH_QUESTION') or '').strip()
    if _q:
        try:
            import research_contract as _RC
            compiled = _RC.compile_contract(_q, use_llm=True, verbose=False)
            print(f'  [contract] compiled from PG_RESEARCH_QUESTION '
                  f'({len(getattr(compiled, "outcome_dimensions", []) or [])} outcome dims, '
                  f'{len(getattr(getattr(compiled, "subject_axis", None), "values", []) or [])} axis values)')
        except Exception as e:                    # fail-safe: subject-free generic frame
            print(f'  [contract] compile failed ({type(e).__name__}: {e}); frame degrades to generic')
    if a.write or a.dry:
        rc = write_report(a.cards, a.graph, a.ledger, a.policy,
                          a.expect_cards_sha, a.expect_graph_sha, a.dry, contract=compiled)
        # HARD EXIT. By here the report is already published atomically (or a dry proof is done). A normal
        # interpreter shutdown would run concurrent.futures' atexit handler, which JOINS every worker
        # thread — including any inner llm() worker abandoned mid-network-read by a 180s timeout. A stuck
        # abandoned worker would hang that join forever (the post-publish freeze), and finalizing the
        # OpenRouter client's leaked async generators on a closed loop has been seen to segfault. os._exit
        # skips both: the process ends immediately with the true return code. stdout/stderr are unbuffered
        # (-u) and flushed here, so no output is lost.
        sys.stdout.flush()
        sys.stderr.flush()
        os._exit(rc)
    print('use --write (or --dry)')

content.text:
I have a complete, reproduced diagnosis. Here is the finding.

---

## Why the ~103 papers are unused

Working set (curated json `manif:HASH` prefixes vs `manif:[0-9a-f]{12}` hits in `glm_reasoning.log`): 285 works total, ~181–207 in the log, ~78–104 absent. Note the log is being **actively appended to by a live compose run** (mtime advanced 08:39 → later during my analysis; the "182/103" in the brief is one snapshot of a moving file). The mechanism below is invariant to that drift.

I reproduced the full pre-writer pipeline against `outputs/compose_inputs/task72_cards_curated.json` + `outputs/provenance_graph.json`. The reason is **not** any of the three filters the brief hypothesized. It is a hardcoded per-subsection card cap.

### It is NOT the faithfulness/source-policy admission gate
`CardBundle.admitted_ids()` (`scripts/report_ast.py:550`, gate in `resolve()` `:465–548`) admits **820 of 838 cards / 279 of 285 works**. Only **6 works (18 cards)** are dropped pre-compose, all by one refusal: `EXPRESSION_KIND_IS_NOT_RENDERABLE_AS_A_CITATION` (`report_ast.py:539–541`) under the `journal_articles_only` policy — i.e. their only expression isn't a citable journal article. There is no tier/peer-review firewall dropping the bulk. So ~98 of the ~103 unused works **pass admission**.

### It is NOT the fact-use ledger partition or a redundancy cap
`fact_use_ledger.build_clusters` demotes only **25 cards** to corroborators (near-duplicate findings) — the corpus is almost all distinct findings, so redundancy capping is negligible. `plan_bundles` (`scripts/fact_use_ledger.py:511–618`) then **narrates every one of the 795 representative findings** — nothing is dropped; an overflow pass (`:562–569`) guarantees each unplaced finding a home. Result: all 279 admitted works are *licensed* to be narrated somewhere. Each of the 26 subsections is licensed **~30–31 findings**.

### THE REAL CAUSE — a per-subsection top-12 truncation in the composer
`scripts/cellcog_composer.py:1101`:
```python
sel = list(dict.fromkeys(sel + _rank(b, licensed, sub, k=12)))[:12]
```
`_rank` (`:929–935`, default `k=12`) keeps only the **12 best-lexical-fit cards per subsection** and the trailing `[:12]` caps it hard. The ledger licenses ~31 findings per subsection but the composer only ever puts **~12** into the writer's prompt. **~19 of ~31 licensed findings per subsection are silently truncated and shown to no writer** — so their work is narrated nowhere, and since only 25 works are corroborators, those works are **cited nowhere in the final report**. 26 × 12 ≈ 312 slots − overlap ≈ 266 distinct cards ≈ ~181–207 distinct works reach a writer. The remainder is the unused ~78–104.

Compounding factor: **143 of 795 representative findings have zero lexical overlap with every subsection title** (`relevance()`, `fact_use_ledger.py:499–508`). Those are placed only by the overflow min-cap dump (`:562–569`) into an arbitrary least-full subsection, where they rank last (relevance 0) and are the first casualties of `[:12]`.

## Are they recoverable GOOD sources? — Mixed; three buckets
Manual inspection of the dropped set shows it is **not uniformly low-value and not uniformly good**:

- **Genuinely off-topic contamination that SHOULD stay out** (the corpus is polluted): lung-cancer mortality (`manif:299bf808a869`, PNAS-family), foreign-aid-corruption AER 2008 (`a44ca9149adc`), social-isolation mortality PNAS 2013 (`dea8647574a0`), Black/White life-expectancy PNAS (`289fbbc32f6d`), LGBTQ+-youth Social Media+Society. Note **16 of the dropped works are in high-reputation venues** — a tier/credibility firewall would *keep* these; relevance, not credibility, is the broken axis.
- **Weak/predatory-venue, on-topic-ish**: Bodhi Intl Journal, İzmir İktisat Dergisi, World Journal of Education & Humanities, ChatGPT-opinion surveys.
- **Genuinely GOOD, on-topic, needlessly excluded**: PNAS nexus 2024 task-impact ("newly impacted tasks, software developers 2016→2019", `4a9410cf2582`), Small Business Economics 2026 Italian-manufacturing employment (`1f578e5d3609`), Philosophy & Technology 2014 service-sector jobs (`790cdc0976ff`), Oeconomia Copernicana 2023 reskilling review (`4deb086d136f`). These are victims purely of the arbitrary cap.

## Is raising coverage toward ~90% safe/worthwhile?
**Not as a blunt lever.** Simply raising `k` (or removing `[:12]`) would recover the good papers **and** pull the off-topic PNAS/AER contamination into the prose — that dilutes the review and can *lower* a relevance-sensitive comprehensiveness/quality judge, not raise it. The dropped set contains real junk that the current cap is (accidentally) suppressing.

The safe, worthwhile fix is **two-sided**:
1. **Relevance floor before composition** — drop the ~9 zero-fit / clearly off-topic reps (health/aid/mortality) up front; they scored relevance 0 for a reason. This is the missing filter — a *topic* firewall, not the *tier* firewall the earlier experiment (correctly) rejected.
2. **Then raise or redistribute the per-subsection cap** (`cellcog_composer.py:1101`) or add a coverage sweep so the ~7+ high-value on-topic dropped papers (PNAS nexus, Small Business Economics, Philosophy & Technology, Oeconomia) actually get narrated once each. Because narration is already a partition, extra licensed findings can be dealt to under-full subsections rather than all fighting for one subsection's top-12.

Net: coverage is being throttled by an arbitrary constant, not by evidence quality. Pushing toward ~90% is worthwhile **only if paired with a relevance floor**; without it, you would be buying comprehensiveness with off-topic noise.

Key file:line references: cap `scripts/cellcog_composer.py:1101` (and `_rank` `:929–935`, `_select` `:278–290`); ledger partition `scripts/fact_use_ledger.py:511–618` (overflow `:562–569`, relevance `:499–508`, clustering `:519`); admission `scripts/report_ast.py:465–551` (only pre-compose drop at `:539–541`). Reproduction scripts left in the session scratchpad (`diag*.py`).
